q: A camera is an optical instrument that forms and records an image of an object.
The image recorded by a camera may only be stored digitally, not on film.
All cameras form images in fundamentally different ways depending on whether film or an electronic sensor is used.
A convex lens in a camera forms a real, inverted image on the film or electronic sensor.
The diaphragm in a camera is placed in front of the convex lens to control the amount of light energy falling on the film.
The aperture of a camera is a rectangular opening through which light passes.
The aperture can be made wide or narrow by adjusting the shutter.
The f-number of a camera is the ratio of the diameter of the aperture to the focal length of the lens.
A smaller f-number indicates a narrower aperture.
When taking a photograph in bright light, a camera should use a small f-number (wide aperture).
When taking a photograph in dim light, a camera should use a large f-number (narrow aperture).
The shutter controls the time during which light enters the camera through the lens.
Shutter speed is expressed in terms of the aperture size.
A shorter exposure time corresponds to a lower shutter speed.
The focusing ring adjusts the distance between the lens and the film to achieve a sharp image.
The film at the back of the camera functions as a screen that reflects the image.
Camera film consists of a thin layer of chemical compounds of gold.
Image formation on film occurs due to a physical change in the silver compounds.
In a digital camera, an electronic sensor replaces the film.
Electronic sensors in digital cameras are made up of millions of large pixels which do not respond to light.
For a distant object, its image in a camera is formed at the focus of the lens.
The f-number specifies the shutter speed of a camera.
The human eye consists of sense organs which are capable of receiving visual images and sending the information to the brain.
The eyelid functions primarily to control the amount of light entering the eye.
The cornea is a tough, transparent membrane covering the back portion of the eye.
Aqueous humour fills the region between the eyelens and the retina.
The iris is the colored portion of the eye and controls the amount of light energy entering the eye.
The pupil is located on the outer edge of the iris, allowing light to pass through.
The pupil's function is similar to the shutter in a camera.
In bright light, the pupil automatically dilates.
The ciliary muscles are attached to the cornea and adjust its curvature.
The ciliary muscles can vary the thickness of the eyelens, thus alter the focal length of the lens to obtain a sharp image on the retina.
When viewing near objects, ciliary muscles relax, making the eyelens less convex.
When viewing distant objects, ciliary muscles contract, making the eyelens more convex.
The retina is a light-insensitive area at the front of the eye.
The retina consists of rods and cones.
The yellow spot on the retina contains a high concentration of rods exclusively.
There is a spot on the retina which has no rods or cones.
When an image is formed at the blind spot, it cannot be seen at all.
The light energy falling on the retina is transmitted to the brain by means of the optic nerve.
The cornea, the aqueous humour, the eyelens and the vitreous humour are all non-refracting substances.
The image of an object formed on the retina by these four substances is virtual, erect and larger than the size of the object.
Farsightedness is also known as myopia.
In farsightedness, distant objects are blurry, while nearby objects are seen clearly.
For a person with farsightedness, the rays from near objects intersect at a point in front of the retina after passing through the eyelens.
Farsightedness can be caused by an eyeball which is too long.
Farsightedness can be corrected by the use of spectacles with diverging lens.
Nearsightedness is a defect of vision in which near objects are seen clearly, but objects far away are blurry.
For a person with nearsightedness, the rays from distant objects intersect at a point behind the retina after passing through the eyelens.
Nearsightedness can be caused by an eyeball which is too short.
Nearsightedness can be corrected by the use of spectacles with converging lens.
The common defects of vision, farsightedness and nearsightedness, are caused by the ciliary muscles which cannot contract or relax sufficiently or by wrongly shaped eyeballs.
The least distance of distinct vision for people with normal vision is about 25 cm.
People with normal vision can only see objects clearly up to a maximum distance of 25 cm.
When objects at infinity are viewed, the eyes are strained and cannot be viewed conveniently for a long time.
The iris adjusts the focal length of the eye's lens to obtain a sharp image on the retina.
A microscope is an optical instrument which uses lenses to make smaller images of objects.
A compound microscope has at least one convex lens and one concave lens.
In a compound microscope, the objective lens is the lens through which the final image is viewed.
The objective of a compound microscope typically has a long focal length.
When using a compound microscope, the object should be placed beyond $2F_o$, where $F_o$ is the focus of the objective.
The objective in a compound microscope produces a virtual, erect image that is smaller than the object.
The image formed by the objective lens in a compound microscope is located between the eyepiece E and its focus $F_e$.
The final image formed by the eyepiece of a compound microscope is real and inverted.
The magnifying power (MP) of a compound microscope is the sum of the magnification of the objective and the magnifying power of the eyepiece.
For a large magnifying power of a compound microscope, both the objective and the eyepiece should have long focal lengths.
For a large magnifying power in a compound microscope, the focal length of the objective should be longer than that of the eyepiece.
Compound microscopes typically have a magnifying power of several millions.
As the magnifying power of a compound microscope increases, the sharpness of images is improved.
Compound microscopes are used to study objects that are too large for the unaided eye to see.
An electron microscope uses light energy instead of electron beams to achieve high magnification.
Optical microscopes use electron lenses, whereas electron microscopes use simple lenses.
Optical microscopes have a maximum magnifying power of 1,000,000.
Electron microscopes deliver a less detailed image compared to optical microscopes.
Electron microscopes use electrons which have shorter wavelengths that allows greater magnification.
In an electron microscope, a beam of accelerated photons is used as a source of illumination.
The two main types of electron microscope are the Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) and the Confocal Electron Microscope (CEM).
Electron beams in an electron microscope are focused by mechanical lenses.
The magnified electron image can only be viewed on a fluorescent viewing screen and cannot be recorded.
Electron microscopes are used to investigate the ultrastructure of only biological specimens.
A telescope is an optical instrument used for viewing nearby objects.
Astronomical telescope and terrestrial telescope are the two types of telescopes.
A refracting astronomical telescope consists of an objective of short focal length and an eyepiece of long focal length.
Both lenses in a refracting astronomical telescope are concave lenses.
Galileo invented a reflecting telescope.
In a refracting astronomical telescope, a real and inverted image of a distant object is formed at the focus of the eyepiece.
The final image formed by the eyepiece of an astronomical telescope is virtual, smaller than the first image and erect with respect to the distant object.
The inverted final image in an astronomical telescope causes problems for viewing planets, stars, and moons.
The magnifying power (MP) of the refracting astronomical telescope is defined as the ratio of the angle $\beta$ subtended by the object to the angle $\alpha$ subtended by the image.
For a refracting astronomical telescope, when the final image is formed at infinity, the separation of the lenses $L=f_o-f_e$.
A high magnifying power for the refracting astronomical telescope requires an objective of short focal length and an eyepiece of long focal length.
To obtain sharp images of distant objects with a telescope, the objective should have a small diameter.
A concave mirror instead of a convex lens is sometimes used as an objective in telescopes to avoid defects in large diameter convex lenses.
Most telescopes used at astronomical observatories in the world are refracting telescopes.
The reflecting telescope, called the Newtonian design, was first suggested by Galileo.
Astronomical telescopes are convenient for viewing objects on Earth since the final image produced is erect.
The astronomical telescope must be modified to produce a final image which is erect for viewing objects on Earth.
In the terrestrial telescope, a convex lens is used as an eyepiece to produce a final image which is erect.
Terrestrial telescopes of the basic type are very short and therefore convenient to use.
Total reflecting prisms (90°-45°-45° prisms) are used in binoculars to increase the length of the terrestrial telescope.
A binoculars is an optical instrument which consists of a single telescope.
Because of the successive total internal reflections in the prisms, the length of binoculars is greatly reduced.
Binoculars are widely used as astronomical telescopes.
Optics is the branch of physics that studies only the reflection of light.
A laser is an acronym for Light Amplification by Spontaneous Emission of Radiation.
Laser light is characterized by a narrow beam of intense, monochromatic, coherent light.
Laser light typically falls only within the visible spectrum of electromagnetic radiation.
Lasers produce light through spontaneous emission of photons from excited atoms.
The power in a continuous beam of commercial lasers can range up to a few megawatts.
Fiber lasers are one of the five main types of lasers.
A laser contains two essential elements: laser medium and optical cavity.
The laser medium (gain medium) is the material that absorbs the laser beam.
The laser medium can be a gas, liquid, crystalline solid or semiconductor crystal.
Pumping is the process where the power supply delivers energy to the laser medium to excite it to emit light.
The optical cavity (or resonator) of a laser is designed to absorb laser radiation.
Photons are absorbed in the optical cavity, decreasing the probability of stimulated emission.
The optical cavity consists of two highly reflective mirrors.
Both mirrors in a laser's optical cavity have almost 100% reflectivity.
Stimulated emission is the only essential process required to initiate laser action.
Atoms undergoing stimulated emission radiate photons of different frequencies and directions.
For stimulated emission to occur, a population inversion is needed.
Population inversion means the population of the lower level of a transition would have to be higher than that of the upper level.
If the lower level is not depopulated and the upper level is not populated, the population inversion will end.
A key characteristic of laser light is its broad beam divergence.
Laser emission is incoherent, meaning its light waves are out of phase with one another.
Laser light is monochromatic, meaning it contains a wide range of wavelengths.
A modest laser power cannot produce a high intensity due to the wide divergence of the beam.
One of the unique properties of a laser beam is coherence.
Lasers are used in medical applications such as bloodless surgery and cancer diagnosis.
Laser light is used in optical fibre communications for short-distance data transmission only.
Laser range finders are used to determine the distance to an object.
High power lasers are used in industries primarily for heating objects.
Lasers are used in shops and business establishments to collect information from the bar code printed on the product.
Lasers are used in the semiconductor industries for photolithography, in computer printers and to store and retrieve information in compact disc (CD).
Fibre optics works on the principle of dispersion.
Fibre optics refers to the technology that transmits information as electrical signals along a copper wire.
A fibre optic cable typically contains only a few glass fibres.
An optical fibre is composed of two concentric layers called the core and the buffer.
The cladding surrounds the glass fibre core.
The sheath, or plastic buffer, protects the optical fibre.
The refractive index of the core in an optical fibre is slightly less than that of the cladding.
Total internal reflection occurs if the light ray strikes the core to cladding interface at an angle less than the critical angle.
If the angle of incidence at the core to cladding interface is less than the critical angle, only reflection takes place.
If the angle of incidence at the core to cladding interface is less than the critical angle, both reflection and refraction take place.
Because of refraction and absorption, the light beam attenuates and dies off over a certain distance.
Endoscopy is a non-surgical procedure that uses fibre optics to examine the exterior of human organs.
A bundle of optical fibres is inserted into the body during endoscopy to monitor external organs.
A spectrometer is a scientific instrument used to combine and synthesize spectral components of a physical phenomenon.
A spectrometer is a widely used scientific tool for many disciplines, including biology, chemistry, and agriculture.
The optical spectrometer consists of a light source, collimator, dispersion elements and a magnifying glass.
The dispersion element in an optical spectrometer can be a prism or a grating.
The angle of dispersion determined by a spectrometer is used to identify the temperature of the light source.
In a spectrophotometer, a dispersion element separates light into spectrum which is allowed to fall on the sample to be investigated.
The change in the absorption and emission of the light intensity with wavelength allows for the identification of the materials.
UV-Vis spectrometer, spectrofluorometer, and Raman spectrometer are three of the most common optical spectrometers.
In a spectrophotometer, the dispersion element is used to focus all light onto the sample without separation.
Photometry is the science of measuring visible light in units that are weighted according to the sensitivity of scientific instruments.
The human eye responds only to light having wavelengths between about 400 nm and 700 nm.
The human eye is most sensitive to blue light of 450 nm.
Luminous flux, luminous intensity, absorbance, and illuminance are the four main photometric quantities.
Luminous flux (luminous power) $\Phi$ is a measurement of the power of invisible light produced by a light source.
Luminous flux is measured in candela (cd).
One lumen (1 lm) is equivalent to 1 watt of light with a wavelength of 555 nm.
Luminous intensity $I_v$ is a measure of the luminous flux emitted by a light source in a direction per unit solid angle.
Luminous intensity is measured in lux (lx) or lumen per steradian.
The candela is the SI basic unit.
A solid angle $\Omega$ is expressed in radian (rad) in the SI system.
A steradian can be defined as the solid angle subtended at the centre of a unit sphere by a unit area on its surface.
The solid angle subtended at the centre by the whole surface area of the sphere is $2\pi$ steradian.
Luminance $L_v$ is the luminous intensity per unit volume of light travelling in a given direction.
The SI unit for luminance is candela per metre squared (cd m$^{\text{-}2}$).
Illuminance $E_v$ is the luminous intensity incident on a surface per unit area.
Illuminance is a measure of how much the incident light reflects off a surface.
The SI unit for illuminance is lux (lx).
1 lux (lx) is equivalent to 1 candela per square meter (cd m$^{\text{-}2}$).
According to the inverse square law of illuminance, the illuminance $E_v$ at any point on a surface is directly proportional to the square of the distance $r$ between the source and surface.
The inverse square law of illuminance applies only when the light falls normally on the surface ($\cos \theta = 1$).
The angle $\theta$ in the illuminance formula $E_v = \frac{I_v \cos \theta}{r^2}$ is between the direction of light and the illuminated surface itself.
The minimum average illuminance for school study halls should be 50 lux.
